stories.headline: Lake County Wildfire West of Leadville Prompts Mandatory Evacuations
stories.summary:
A wildfire burning in Lake County west of Leadville has prompted authorities to issue mandatory evacuation orders on Sunday, June 29. The fire represents the latest in a series of wildland blazes threatening Colorado communities as fire danger surges across the state.

The evacuation orders affect residents in the path of the fire, which is burning in a region already under elevated fire risk due to drought conditions. Lake, Eagle and Pitkin counties have been among the hardest hit by drought this season.

The Red Cross of Western Colorado has also opened an evacuation center in the region for residents displaced by the Snyder fire, a separate blaze affecting Grand Junction-area communities, underscoring the breadth of the ongoing wildfire threat across western Colorado.

stories.headline: Democratic Primary Battle for Denver's House District 6 Becomes Most Expensive House Race in Colorado History
stories.summary:
The contest between incumbent Rep. Sean Camacho and challenger Iris Halpern for House District 6 has surpassed $1.2 million in total spending, making it almost certainly the most expensive Colorado House primary on record. The district, which runs east from Capitol Hill to the Aurora border, is one of the safest Democratic seats in the state — yet has hosted three costly primary contests since redistricting in 2022.

Combined spending across the three most recent primaries in the district has reached roughly $2.5 million, with the bulk coming from dark-money groups backing more moderate candidates. Business interests that once focused on competitive districts are increasingly directing money into safe Democratic seats as the party consolidates one-party control of Colorado.

The broader pattern extends beyond District 6: roughly $3 million has been spent across seven Democratic legislative primaries this cycle, analysts say. Political experts note the spending reflects widening ideological divisions within the Colorado Democratic Party, with moderate and progressive factions competing intensely even in noncompetitive general-election districts.

Long-time district resident Donna Smith, who herself ran a House campaign in 1986 for $12,000 in today's dollars, expressed frustration at the scale of spending. "It's scary, right now, to think that we're going to waste that money, at this time, on these stupid attacks, when there's so much work that needs to be done," Smith said.

stories.headline: Colorado Sun Columnist Argues Attorney General Race Illustrates Need for Ranked Choice Voting
stories.summary:
With Tuesday's Democratic primary for Colorado attorney general drawing near, Colorado Sun columnist Mario Nicolais argues the race is a textbook illustration of why Colorado should adopt ranked choice voting. Former Secretary of State Jena Griswold is widely expected to win the primary with a plurality — potentially in the low-to-mid 40 percent range — meaning most Democratic voters will have backed someone else.

Nicolais contends that three opponents with stronger legal backgrounds — Boulder DA Michael Dougherty, consumer rights attorney David Seligman, and former DOJ official Hetal Doshi — are likely to split the remaining vote and cancel each other out. He argues that under a ranked choice system, one of those candidates could consolidate anti-Griswold support and potentially prevail.

Griswold has significantly outraised her opponents, bringing in nearly a million dollars more than each rival, while also benefiting from high name recognition after eight years as secretary of state. The column comes the day before the June 30 primary, in which the Democratic attorney general nominee will almost certainly face a Republican challenger in November.

stories.headline: Nikola Jokic Reiterates Long-Term Commitment to Denver Nuggets Ahead of NBA Free Agency
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokic has reaffirmed his desire to remain with the franchise long-term, according to a report from Hoops Hype's Mike Scotto, quelling concerns that had emerged this offseason about his future in Denver. Jokic, a three-time NBA MVP widely regarded as the best player in the world, has a player option for the 2027-28 season.

The reassurance comes as the Nuggets front office evaluates moves to improve the roster around Jokic. Multiple reports indicate the team is weighing a blockbuster trade to pair Jokic with an All-NBA wing, while also monitoring re-signing restricted free agent Peyton Watson, whose price tag in free agency could prove significant.

The Nuggets also made a flurry of undrafted free agent signings in the days following the 2026 NBA Draft as they continue to build depth around their franchise cornerstone heading into the offseason.

stories.headline: Colorado Avalanche Acquire Fabian Lysell from Boston Bruins, Make Nine Selections at 2026 NHL Draft
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche were active at the 2026 NHL Draft in Buffalo, making nine total selections while also executing a trade with the Boston Bruins to acquire forward Fabian Lysell. Lysell, a 2021 first-round pick, is a Swedish winger who has yet to fully translate his prospect pedigree into consistent NHL production.

Among Colorado's draft picks was defender Beckett Hamilton, selected 74th overall in the fifth round. The Avalanche also used picks acquired in recent trades — including assets obtained in the Valeri Nichushkin and Jack Drury deals — to replenish their prospect pool.

With the draft activity, Colorado's projected cap space stands at approximately $5.6 million against a cap hit of $98.4 million, leaving limited but present room for further offseason moves. The Avalanche have already signed defensemen Brent Burns and Brett Kulak to new contracts this offseason.

stories.headline: Rockies Hitters Grapple with Historic Velocity Surge as Pitchers Reach New Speed Milestones
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies first baseman Kyle Karros and hitting coach Brett Pill are among those navigating what analysts describe as the most difficult hitting environment in the history of professional baseball. The average four-seam fastball has climbed from 89.0 mph in 2002 to 94.4 mph as of May 2026, and Milwaukee's Jacob Misiorowski recently threw the hardest recorded pitch by a starting pitcher in league history at 105.5 mph.

Karros, son of 1992 NL Rookie of the Year Eric Karros, says conversations with his father have taken on a generational flavor, with the younger Karros pushing back on suggestions that hitting has always been this hard. "You weren't facing 99 miles an hour with sink," Kyle told his father. The Rockies have incorporated Trajekt pitching simulation technology to help hitters prepare for elite velocity and movement.

Pitching coach and former big leaguer Brett Pill said the evolution was jarring even for a player who retired only a few years ago. When he returned to spring training in 2017 after three years in the KBO League, the velocity jump in just a short span was immediately noticeable. The Rockies faced the challenge head-on during a recent Father's Day series against Pittsburgh, squaring off against reigning Cy Young winner Paul Skenes.

stories.headline: Thousands Attend Denver Pride Parade and PrideFest to Close Out Pride Month
stories.summary:
Thousands of people gathered in downtown Denver on Sunday to celebrate the annual Denver PrideFest and Pride Parade, closing out Pride Month with events highlighting the LGBTQ+ community. The parade returned to 17th Avenue, featuring local businesses, community organizations, colorful floats and performers marching west toward downtown.

Following the parade, festivities moved to 16th Street for PrideFest, an event that has brought together LGBTQ+ community members, allies, families and businesses since 1974. Performers Nini Coco, Pattie Gonia and King Molasses headlined the Center Stage lineup, alongside longtime festival attractions including the Latin Stage, the Gayborhood and a youth and family area.

The events were hosted by The Center on Colfax, the largest LGBTQ+ community center in the Rocky Mountain region. Organizers called Denver PrideFest one of the largest annual celebrations of the LGBTQ+ community in Colorado, serving as both a celebration and a platform for visibility and advocacy.

stories.headline: Boulder County Childcare Costs Nearly Double State Average as Government Support Dwindles
stories.summary:
Childcare costs in Boulder County are running at nearly double the Colorado state average, according to reporting from the Colorado Sun, placing significant financial pressure on families in one of the state's most expensive housing markets. Providers in the county are actively seeking new revenue sources as government assistance programs for families face reductions.

The situation reflects a broader statewide challenge. Colorado has made childcare affordability a legislative priority in recent years, but providers and families say the gap between costs and available support continues to widen, particularly in high-cost communities along the Front Range.

The Colorado Sun has been pursuing a solutions journalism series examining the state's childcare system, exploring both the scale of the problem and potential policy and private-sector responses. Boulder County's situation is being highlighted as an acute example of the affordability pressures facing Colorado families.

stories.headline: Fort Garland Exhibit Explores Adobe's Deep Roots in Southern Colorado History
stories.summary:
The Fort Garland Cultural and Historic Center on the eastern edge of the San Luis Valley has opened a new joint exhibition, "Unearthing Futures/Desenterrando Futuras," that uses adobe construction as a lens to examine the deep history of southern Colorado — history that predates both Colorado's 150th anniversary as a state and the nation's 250th birthday.

The exhibition is co-hosted with the Harwood Museum in Taos, New Mexico, and features works by contemporary adobe artists including rafa esparza, Christine Howard Sandoval and Ronald Rael. An accompanying app maps road trips to significant adobe sites across southern Colorado and northern New Mexico.

Fort Garland director Eric Carpio said the dual anniversary year offered a chance to remind visitors that the history of the San Luis Valley extends far beyond 1876 or even 1776. Five of the fort's original adobe buildings, constructed in 1858, still stand on the site. "Adobe is a beautiful metaphor for that," Carpio said, describing it as a compression of cultures and conditions that shaped the region over millennia.

stories.headline: Hazardous Materials Spill Causes Traffic Backups on South Metro Denver Highway
stories.summary:
A hazardous materials spill on a south metro Denver highway is causing significant traffic backups in the area, according to the Denver Post. The incident prompted emergency response and lane closures, affecting commuters and drivers in the southern part of the metro area.

Details on the specific materials involved and the cause of the spill were not immediately available. Authorities were on scene managing the response as of Sunday afternoon.

Drivers in the south metro area were advised to seek alternate routes while the cleanup was underway. No injuries were immediately reported.

stories.headline: Arvada Author John Andrews Spotlights WWI Nurses in New Historical Novel
stories.summary:
Retired critical care physician and Arvada resident John Andrews has published a new historical novel, "An American Nurse in Paris," as part of his Novels of the Great War Series. The book follows medical journalist Alice Simmons, whose efforts to become a war correspondent in France are blocked by sexism in the U.S. Army and American press corps, leading her to take up nursing in a Red Cross military hospital during the final year of World War I.

Andrews said the novel was inspired by the largely unrecognized contributions of American Red Cross and U.S. military nurses during WWI. He also served as a volunteer firefighter and EMT before turning to fiction writing, and has previously written books aimed at U.S. Marines about the Battle of Belleau Wood.

The book is featured in the Colorado Sun's SunLit series, a weekly collaboration with Colorado Humanities & Center For The Book that highlights new work by Colorado authors. Andrews lives in Arvada and the novel is the latest in his ongoing World War I fiction series.

stories.headline: Fourth of July Fireworks Displays Scheduled Across Denver Metro for Independence Day Weekend
stories.summary:
Fireworks displays and Independence Day celebrations are scheduled at multiple locations across the Denver metro area for the July 4th weekend, as the nation marks its 250th anniversary. A comprehensive guide to events has been published for residents planning to attend community celebrations.

The holiday weekend coincides with the America 250 national commemoration, and several local municipalities and event organizers have planned larger-than-usual displays. Specific venue details, start times and parking information were included in the metro guide.

Residents are advised to check local event listings for updates, as weather and fire conditions may affect some outdoor celebrations given the ongoing drought across the Front Range.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I don't think you should have to pay for a library card. That's the whole point. The books are free. Now the card costs money? What's next, a fee for breathing?

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Agree to Stand Down After Exchange of Strikes, Nuclear Talks Remain on Track
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran have agreed to halt military strikes and restore free passage through the Strait of Hormuz following a series of escalating attacks that threatened to unravel a ceasefire signed less than two weeks ago, U.S. officials confirmed Monday. A senior Trump administration official told ABC News that technical talks aimed at implementing a memorandum of understanding between the two countries are still planned for the coming days and that no negotiations have been canceled.

The latest round of hostilities began Thursday when an Iranian projectile struck a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz. The U.S. retaliated over the weekend with strikes on multiple targets inside Iran, which U.S. Central Command described as a direct response to continued aggression against commercial shipping. Iran then launched missiles and drones at U.S. bases in Kuwait and Bahrain on Sunday, but U.S. officials said all projectiles were shot down, intercepted, or failed to reach their targets, and that there were no American casualties or damage to U.S. facilities.

The exchange threatened to collapse a 14-point Memorandum of Understanding signed by both countries on June 17, which called for an immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts. Under that agreement, Iran had committed to using its best efforts to ensure the safe passage of commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz at no charge for a period of 60 days. Delegations from both countries met last weekend at the Bürgenstock resort in Switzerland to begin negotiations on a broader war-ending deal.

The Strait of Hormuz carries a significant share of global oil and gas shipments and had been effectively closed by Tehran after the U.S. and Israel launched major strikes on Iran at the end of February. President Donald Trump announced what he called major combat operations against Iran on Feb. 28, with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes targeting military, government, and infrastructure sites across the country.

Complications also emerged on a separate front, where an Israel-Lebanon framework agreement brokered by the U.S. on Friday faces resistance. Hezbollah's leader rejected the accord and accused the Beirut government of undermining Lebanese sovereignty, while the Israeli military on Sunday struck a tunnel it said was more than 200 meters long and 25 meters deep in southern Lebanon, containing hundreds of weapons and several launch silos. Israel said it notified the United States in advance of the strike, and Iran has stated that a cessation of hostilities in Lebanon is a prerequisite for any broader regional ceasefire to hold.
